title: Two words for two things
summary: A poet who wrote that the tech industry has 'killed the word attention' meets the attention researcher who felt called out by his essay. They debate whether the dwell-time number and the older meaning are even the same thing.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Bác Hoàng, I read your essay last week. The one where you wrote that we have 'killed' the word 'attention'. I'm an attention researcher. I felt you were talking about me.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: Linh, I am not in the habit of writing about specific people I have not met. But yes — perhaps you and I should talk.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Tell me how I have killed the word.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: You haven't, personally. The field has. The word 'attention', for centuries, meant something close to the act of really regarding another being or thing. A mother attends to her child. A reader attends to a poem. To attend is to give of yourself — your awareness — to what is in front of you. Then your industry took the word and gave it to a stopwatch.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: We had to measure something. If attention is what we are selling, we needed numbers.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: Yes. And the numbers are real. I am not denying that. The question is whether what you measure is what the original word meant. Dwell time, scrolls, clicks — these are records of behaviour. They tell you whether a body remained near a screen. They do not tell you whether a mind was present.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Is there even a difference, in practice? If someone spends ten minutes on a page, attention has happened.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: Has it? I have spent ten minutes on a phone in a doctor's waiting room without reading a word. I was looking at the screen because I was avoiding the room. That is not attention. That is the body's politeness while the mind is elsewhere.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: But if we cannot tell the difference, why does the difference matter? Companies cannot pay for an internal state they cannot detect.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: That is the most honest thing you have said. Companies cannot. So companies will pay for the visible thing — and call it the real thing. And eventually the language will move. People will believe the new meaning, because no other meaning has a metric. And then we will have lost the word.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: And then what? What does the world lose, in your view, when 'attention' just means 'eyes on screen'?

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: It loses the practice the old word pointed at. The slow practice of staying, of receiving, of letting something not yet understood remain in your awareness without rushing to a verdict. You cannot build that with a counter. It builds itself, with time, in a person who knows they are giving the thing.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Suppose I take this seriously. What does my field do? Stop measuring?

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: No. Measure. Measurement is fine. But change the label. Call your number 'engagement', or 'dwell', or 'visibility'. Reserve 'attention' for the older meaning. Then we will at least know what we have, and what we have lost.

body.speaker: Hoàng
body.text: Two words for two things. It is the cheapest reform I can think of, and the hardest to pass.

annotations.meaningVi: lưu lại, ở lại (trên một trang)
mức độ tương tác (thuật ngữ trong ngành công nghệ)
cải cách, sự thay đổi có hệ thống

grammarNotes.title: Hypothetical 'Suppose…' to invite consideration
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tranh luận, 'Suppose I…' (Giả sử tôi…) là cách lịch sự để mời người đối diện cùng xem xét một khả năng mà bản thân chưa cam kết. Trong bài: 'Suppose I take this seriously. What does my field do?' Cấu trúc này nhẹ hơn 'If I take this seriously, then…' và mang tính khám phá hơn — Linh chưa hứa làm gì, chỉ đang xem chuyện gì sẽ xảy ra nếu cô ấy chấp nhận lập luận của Hoàng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In argument, 'Suppose I…' invites the other person to consider a possibility you haven't yet committed to. From the dialogue: 'Suppose I take this seriously. What does my field do?' It is softer than 'If I take this seriously, then…' — more exploratory. Linh isn't promising anything yet; she's testing what would follow if she accepted Hoàng's argument.

cloze.template:
Linh: Bác Hoàng, I read your essay last week. The one where you wrote that we have 'killed' the word 'attention'. I'm an attention {{b1}}. I felt you were talking about me.
Hoàng: Linh, I am not in the habit of writing about specific people I have not met. But yes — perhaps you and I should talk.
Linh: Tell me how I have killed the word.
Hoàng: You haven't, personally. The field has. The word 'attention', for centuries, meant something close to the act of really {{b2}} another being or thing. A mother attends to her child. A reader attends to a poem. To attend is to give of yourself — your {{b3}} — to what is in front of you. Then your industry took the word and gave it to a {{b4}}.
Linh: We had to {{b5}} something. If attention is what we are selling, we needed numbers.
Hoàng: Yes. And the numbers are real. I am not denying that. The question is whether what you measure is what the original word meant. Dwell time, scrolls, clicks — these are records of {{b6}}. They tell you whether a body remained near a screen. They do not tell you whether a mind was present.
Linh: Is there even a difference, in practice? If someone spends ten minutes on a page, attention has happened.
Hoàng: Has it? I have spent ten minutes on a phone in a doctor's {{b7}} room without reading a word. I was looking at the screen because I was avoiding the room. That is not attention. That is the body's {{b8}} while the mind is elsewhere.
Linh: But if we cannot tell the difference, why does the difference matter? Companies cannot pay for an internal state they cannot {{b9}}.
Hoàng: That is the most honest thing you have said. Companies cannot. So companies will pay for the visible thing — and call it the real thing. And eventually the language will move. People will believe the new meaning, because no other meaning has a {{b10}}. And then we will have lost the word.
Linh: And then what? What does the world lose, in your view, when 'attention' just means 'eyes on screen'?
Hoàng: It loses the practice the old word pointed at. The slow practice of staying, of receiving, of letting something not yet understood remain in your awareness without rushing to a {{b11}}. You cannot build that with a {{b12}}. It builds itself, with time, in a person who knows they are giving the thing.
Linh: Suppose I take this seriously. What does my field do? Stop measuring?
Hoàng: No. Measure. Measurement is fine. But change the label. Call your number '{{b13}}', or 'dwell', or 'visibility'. Reserve 'attention' for the {{b14}} meaning. Then we will at least know what we have, and what we have lost.
Linh: Two words for two things.
Hoàng: Two words for two things. It is the cheapest {{b15}} I can think of, and the {{b16}} to pass.

blanks.explanation: Linh introduces herself as an attention researcher — that is why she felt the essay was about her.
The old meaning of 'attention': really regarding another being or thing.
To attend is to give of yourself — your awareness — to what is in front of you.
Your industry gave the word to a stopwatch — measurement replaced meaning.
Linh's defence: 'We had to measure something.'
Dwell time, scrolls, clicks are records of behaviour — what a body did, not what a mind experienced.
Hoàng's example: ten minutes on a phone in a doctor's waiting room without reading a word.
'The body's politeness while the mind is elsewhere' — staying near the screen out of social or situational habit, not attention.
Linh: companies cannot pay for an internal state they cannot detect — her sharpest economic counter.
No other meaning has a metric — and so the metricised meaning wins by default.
Letting something remain in your awareness without rushing to a verdict — refusing premature judgement.
You cannot build it with a counter — a measuring device cannot produce the slow practice Hoàng describes.
Call your number 'engagement' — a label that does not steal the older word.
Reserve 'attention' for the older meaning — keep the original word for the original idea.
It is 'the cheapest reform I can think of' — costless in money, very expensive in habit.
And the hardest to pass — because the industry's incentives push against it.

translationVi:
Linh: Bác Hoàng, tuần trước cháu đọc bài của bác. Bài bác viết rằng chúng ta đã 'giết chết' từ 'chú ý'. Cháu là người nghiên cứu về chú ý. Cháu cảm giác bác đang nói về cháu.
Hoàng: Linh, bác không có thói quen viết về một người cụ thể mà bác chưa gặp. Nhưng đúng — có lẽ cháu và bác nên nói chuyện.
Linh: Bác kể cho cháu nghe cháu đã giết từ ấy thế nào.
Hoàng: Không phải cháu cá nhân. Là cả ngành. Từ 'chú ý', trong nhiều thế kỷ, có nghĩa gần với hành động thực sự để mắt đến một sinh thể hay vật thể khác. Một bà mẹ chú ý đến đứa con. Một độc giả chú ý đến một bài thơ. Chú ý là tặng đi của chính mình — sự ý thức của mình — cho cái đang ở trước mặt. Rồi ngành của cháu lấy từ đó và đưa nó cho một cái đồng hồ bấm giờ.
Linh: Chúng cháu phải đo cái gì đó chứ. Nếu chú ý là cái chúng cháu đang bán, thì chúng cháu cần con số.
Hoàng: Đúng. Và những con số là có thật. Bác không phủ nhận điều đó. Câu hỏi là cái cháu đo có phải là cái mà từ gốc ấy đã có nghĩa hay không. Thời gian ở lại trên trang, số lần cuộn, số lần nhấp — đó là ghi chép của hành vi. Chúng nói cho cháu biết một thân thể đã ở gần một màn hình hay không. Chúng không nói cho cháu biết một tâm trí đã có mặt hay không.
Linh: Trên thực tế có khác biệt không bác? Nếu ai đó ở trên một trang mười phút, chú ý đã xảy ra.
Hoàng: Đã xảy ra không? Bác từng ở mười phút trên điện thoại trong phòng chờ bác sĩ mà không đọc một chữ nào. Bác nhìn vào màn hình vì bác muốn tránh căn phòng. Đó không phải là chú ý. Đó là sự lịch sự của thân thể khi tâm trí đang ở nơi khác.
Linh: Nhưng nếu chúng cháu không phân biệt được, vì sao khác biệt đó lại quan trọng? Các công ty không thể trả tiền cho một trạng thái nội tâm mà họ không phát hiện được.
Hoàng: Đó là điều thành thật nhất cháu nói nãy giờ. Các công ty không thể. Vì thế các công ty sẽ trả tiền cho cái nhìn thấy được — và gọi nó là cái thật. Và rồi ngôn ngữ sẽ trôi đi. Người ta sẽ tin vào nghĩa mới, vì không có nghĩa nào khác có chỉ số đo. Và đến lúc đó, chúng ta đã đánh mất cái từ.
Linh: Và rồi sao nữa? Theo bác, thế giới mất gì khi 'chú ý' chỉ còn nghĩa là 'mắt nhìn màn hình'?
Hoàng: Nó mất đi cái thực hành mà từ cũ đang chỉ tới. Thực hành chậm rãi của việc ở lại, của việc tiếp nhận, của việc để một thứ chưa hiểu được nằm trong ý thức mình mà không vội vã đưa ra phán quyết. Cháu không xây được điều đó bằng một bộ đếm. Nó tự xây trong một con người biết rằng họ đang trao thứ ấy.
Linh: Giả sử cháu coi điều này nghiêm túc. Ngành của cháu nên làm gì? Ngừng đo?
Hoàng: Không. Cứ đo. Đo là chuyện tốt. Nhưng đổi nhãn. Gọi con số của cháu là 'mức tương tác', hay 'thời gian dừng lại', hay 'độ hiển thị'. Để dành từ 'chú ý' cho nghĩa cũ. Khi đó ít nhất ta cũng biết mình đang có gì, và đã mất gì.
Linh: Hai từ cho hai thứ.
Hoàng: Hai từ cho hai thứ. Đó là cuộc cải cách rẻ nhất bác có thể nghĩ ra, và khó thuyết phục ngành nhất.

questions.prompt: Why does Linh feel Hoàng's essay was about her?
options: She is an attention researcher, and the essay claimed her field has 'killed' the word 'attention'
questions.explanation: 'I'm an attention researcher. I felt you were talking about me.'

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng say the word 'attention' meant for centuries?
options: Something close to the act of really regarding another being or thing — giving your awareness
questions.explanation: 'The word "attention"… meant something close to the act of really regarding another being or thing.'
questions.hint: Look at Hoàng's long first explanation.

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng say Linh's industry did with the word 'attention'?
options: Took the word and gave it to a stopwatch — to measurement
questions.explanation: 'Then your industry took the word and gave it to a stopwatch.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of Hoàng's first long turn.

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng say dwell time, scrolls, and clicks measure?
options: What a body did — whether it remained near a screen, not whether a mind was present
questions.explanation: 'These are records of behaviour. They tell you whether a body remained near a screen. They do not tell you whether a mind was present.'
questions.hint: Look at Hoàng's turn that begins 'Yes. And the numbers are real.'

questions.prompt: What example does Hoàng give of being on a screen without paying attention?
options: Ten minutes on a phone in a doctor's waiting room without reading a word
questions.explanation: 'I have spent ten minutes on a phone in a doctor's waiting room without reading a word.'
questions.hint: Look at Hoàng's turn about the waiting room.

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng call this kind of looking at a screen?
options: The body's politeness while the mind is elsewhere
questions.explanation: 'That is the body's politeness while the mind is elsewhere.'
questions.hint: Same turn as the previous question.

questions.prompt: What is Linh's strongest economic counter-argument?
options: Companies cannot pay for an internal state they cannot detect
questions.explanation: Hoàng even concedes: 'That is the most honest thing you have said.'
questions.hint: Look at Linh's turn about companies and internal states.

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng say will eventually happen to the language?
options: People will believe the new meaning of 'attention', because no other meaning has a metric — and the word will be lost
It will split into two languages
questions.explanation: 'People will believe the new meaning, because no other meaning has a metric. And then we will have lost the word.'
questions.hint: Look at Hoàng's turn that begins 'That is the most honest thing you have said.'

questions.prompt: According to Hoàng, what does the world lose when 'attention' just means 'eyes on screen'?
options: The slow practice of staying, of receiving, of letting something not yet understood remain in your awareness without rushing to a verdict
questions.explanation: 'The slow practice of staying, of receiving, of letting something not yet understood remain in your awareness without rushing to a verdict.'
questions.hint: Look at Hoàng's turn after Linh asks 'What does the world lose…'

questions.prompt: Does Hoàng tell Linh's field to stop measuring?
options: No — he says 'Measure. Measurement is fine.' but to change the label
questions.explanation: 'No. Measure. Measurement is fine. But change the label.'

questions.prompt: What practical reform does Hoàng propose?
options: Call the number 'engagement', 'dwell', or 'visibility', and reserve 'attention' for the older meaning
questions.explanation: 'Call your number "engagement", or "dwell", or "visibility". Reserve "attention" for the older meaning.'

questions.prompt: What does Hoàng mean by 'the cheapest reform I can think of, and the hardest to pass'?
options: It costs a lot of money but is easy
It costs nothing materially, but the industry's habits will resist the new vocabulary
questions.explanation: Two words for two things — no real cost — but changing how a profession speaks is difficult.
questions.hint: Look at the very last turn.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is closer to Hoàng's central claim?
options: Measurement is bad and must end.
Measurement is fine, but the metric and the older idea are different things and deserve different names.
questions.explanation: 'No. Measure. Measurement is fine. But change the label.' He preserves the practical activity while protecting the word.
questions.hint: Look at the proposal Hoàng actually makes.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Hoàng NOT claiming?
options: That dwell time is a real number
That dwell time and the older idea of attention are the same thing
That a 'mind present' state is different from a 'body present' state
That the language may move because the metric wins by default
questions.explanation: His central distinction is that dwell time measures behaviour, not mind-presence. He explicitly denies they are the same.
questions.hint: Look at his turn about records of behaviour.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the exchange?
options: Polite but unproductive — they talk past each other
Honest and probing — each treats the other's strongest argument seriously and reaches a concrete proposal
questions.explanation: Hoàng calls Linh's counter 'the most honest thing you have said'; Linh accepts the framing 'two words for two things'.
questions.hint: Notice how each responds to the other's strongest move.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hoàng suggests that when an old word is captured by a measurement, the practice the word pointed at quietly disappears. Think of another everyday word that has, in your lifetime, been reduced to a number — 'friend', 'view', 'like', 'success'. What practice did the original word once point at, and is it still possible to do that practice today?
